1: רָחַשׁ לִבִּי דָּבָר טוֹב (rāḥash libbî dābār ṭôb, "My heart is overflowing with a beautiful theme") — rāḥash: a hapax legomenon (appears only here) — to bubble up, to overflow, to boil over (like a pot). The heart as the vessel from which the beautiful word pours. אֹמֵר אָנִי מַעֲשַׂי לְמֶלֶךְ (ʾōmêr ʾānî maʿăśay lĕmelek, "I address my poem to the king") — a wedding ode for the king; maʿăśay: my work(s), my compositions. לְשׁוֹנִי עֵט סֹפֵר מָהִיר (lĕshônî ʿêṭ sōpêr māhîr, "my tongue is the pen of a skilled scribe") — the tongue as a writing instrument, the poet as a skilled copyist (māhîr: swift, expert). The superscription: "A Song of Loves" (shîr yĕdîdōt) — yĕdîdāh: beloved, darling (the same root as yĕdîdîm, the covenant love-name).
2: יָפְיָפִיתָ מִבְּנֵי אָדָם (yāpyāpîtā mibbĕnê ʾādām, "You are more handsome than any other man") — yāpāh (doubled for intensity): the most beautiful among Adam's sons. הוּצַק חֵן בְּשִׂפְתוֹתֶיךָ (hûṣaq ḥên bĕśiptôtêkā, "grace is poured into your lips") — yāṣaq (hophal: to be poured out): grace (ḥên) as liquid poured into the king's speech. עַל-כֵּן בֵּרַכְךָ אֱלֹהִים לְעוֹלָם (ʿal-kên bêrakĕkā ʾĕlōhîm lĕʿôlām, "therefore God has blessed you forever") — the beauty and gracious speech are not independent gifts but expressions of the divine blessing of the anointed king.
3: חֲגוֹר-חַרְבְּךָ עַל-יָרֵךְ גִּבּוֹר (ḥăgôr-ḥarbĕkā ʿal-yārêk gibbôr, "Strap your sword on your thigh, O champion") — ḥāgar: to gird, to belt; gibbôr: the mighty warrior-hero. הוֹדְךָ וַהֲדָרֶךָ (hôdĕkā wahădārekā, "your glory and splendor") — hôd and hādār: the divine glory-vocabulary of Psalms 21:5, now applied to the warrior-king. The warrior's military accoutrements are described in the language of divine splendor — the king as the earthly bearer of divine glory.
4: וַהֲדָרְךָ צְלַח רְכַב (wahădārkā ṣĕlaḥ rĕkab, "Ride out victoriously in your splendor") — ṣālaḥ: to prosper, to succeed (the cavalry image). עַל-דְּבַר-אֱמֶת וְעַנְוָה-צֶדֶק (ʿal-dĕbar-ʾĕmet wĕʿanwāh-ṣedeq, "for the sake of truth, humility, and righteousness") — the three foundations of the ideal kingship: ʾĕmet (truth/faithfulness), ʿanwāh (meekness/humility — the same word used of Moses in Numbers 12:3 and of the meek who inherit the earth in Matthew 5:5), and ṣedeq (righteousness). וְתוֹרְךָ נוֹרָאוֹת יְמִינֶךָ (wĕtôrĕkā nôrāʾôt yĕmînekā, "let your right hand achieve awesome deeds") — yārah (hiphil: to teach/direct — but here the sense is "to show, to point out"): the right hand teaching awesome things through the deeds of war.
5: חִצֶּיךָ שְׁנוּנִים עַמִּים תַּחְתֶּיךָ יִפְּלוּ (ḥiṣṣêkā shĕnûnîm ʿammîm taḥtêkā yippĕlû, "Your arrows are sharp, bringing peoples down beneath you") — shānān: to sharpen (the whetted arrows of the divine warrior, now the king's arrows). Yippĕlû taḥtêkā: "they fall beneath you" — the posture of the defeated before the victor (Psalms 18:38-39). בְּלֵב אוֹיְבֵי הַמֶּלֶךְ (bĕlêb ʾôyĕbê hammelek, "they pierce into the hearts of the king's enemies") — the arrows strike to the heart, not merely the body's surface.
6: כִּסְאֲךָ אֱלֹהִים עוֹלָם וָעֶד (kisʾăkā ʾĕlōhîm ʿôlām wāʿed, "Your throne, O God, is forever and ever") — one of the most theologically contested verses in the OT. The king is addressed as ʾĕlōhîm (God). Three main readings: (1) "Your throne is God's throne forever" — the ʾĕlōhîm as a genitive of possession; (2) "God is your throne forever" — ʾĕlōhîm as predicate; (3) the Psalmist directly addresses the king as ʾĕlōhîm — applying divine language to the anointed representative. The LXX reads it as direct address: ὁ θρόνος σου ὁ θεός ("your throne, O God"). Hebrews 1:8 quotes this verse and applies it to Christ: "But about the Son he says, 'Your throne, O God, will last for ever and ever.'" The NT's authoritative interpretation establishes this as Messianic — the eternal throne of the Son, not merely of a historical Davidic king. שֵׁבֶט מִישֹׁר שֵׁבֶט מַלְכוּתֶךָ (shêbeṭ mîshōr shêbeṭ malkûtekā, "the scepter of your kingdom is a righteous scepter") — shêbeṭ: the royal scepter (the symbol of authority and judgment); mîshōr: uprightness, justice (the level/straight as the standard of just governance).
7: אָהַבְתָּ צֶּדֶק וַתִּשְׂנָא רֶשַׁע (ʾāhabtā ṣedeq wattishnāʾ reshaʿ, "You have loved righteousness and hated wickedness") — the moral character of the anointed king: active love for ṣedeq (righteousness/justice) and active hatred for reshaʿ (wickedness). The pairing: love and hate as the two poles of moral commitment. עַל-כֵּן מְשָׁחֲךָ אֱלֹהִים אֱלֹהֶיךָ שֶׁמֶן שָׂשׂוֹן מֵחֲבֵרֶיךָ (ʿal-kên mĕshāḥăkā ʾĕlōhîm ʾĕlōhêkā shemen śāśôn mêḥăbêrêkā, "therefore God, your God, has anointed you with the oil of gladness beyond all your companions") — māshaḥ: to anoint (the verb behind māshîaḥ/Messiah); shemen śāśôn: "oil of gladness/exultation." The anointing with joy-oil beyond all others. Hebrews 1:9 quotes this verse of Christ as the uniquely anointed one whose joy-anointing exceeds all others.
8: מֹר-וַאֲהָלוֹת קְצִיעוֹת כָּל-בִּגְדֹתֶיךָ (mōr-waʾăhālôt qĕṣîʿôt kol-bigdōtêkā, "All your robes are fragrant with myrrh, aloes, and cassia") — mōr: myrrh (the prized resin used in the anointing oil, Exodus 30:23); ʾăhālôt: aloes (a fragrant wood); qĕṣîʿāh: cassia (cinnamon-like spice). The three spices of the anointing oil and the divine presence concentrated in the royal garments. מִן-הֵיכְלֵי שֵׁן (min-hêkĕlê shên, "from ivory palaces") — shên: ivory (the luxury material of the royal palace, as in Ahab's ivory house, 1 Kings 22:39). Music (minnî) bringing joy from within the palace of ivory.
9: בְּנוֹת מְלָכִים בְּיִקְּרוֹתֶיךָ (bĕnôt mĕlākîm bĕyiqqĕrôtêkā, "Daughters of kings are among your ladies of honor") — the international scope of the royal court. נִצְּבָה שֵׁגַל לִימִינְךָ בְּכֶתֶם אוֹפִיר (niṣṣĕbāh shêgal lîmînĕkā bĕketem ʾôpîr, "at your right hand stands the queen arrayed in gold of Ophir") — shêgal: the queen/consort; ketem ʾôpîr: Ophir-gold — the finest gold of the ancient world (1 Kings 9:28; Job 28:16). Positioned at the king's right hand — the place of honor.
10: שִׁמְעִי-בַת וּרְאִי וְהַטִּי אָזְנֵךְ (shîmʿî-bat ûrĕʾî wĕhaṭṭî ʾāznêk, "Listen, daughter, and pay attention; lean in with your ear") — the address turns to the bride: "Listen, daughter" — bat used as a term of address for the queen. The three imperatives of attentive reception: hear, see, incline the ear. וְשִׁכְחִי עַמֵּךְ וּבֵית אָבִיךְ (wĕshikhĕhî ʿammêk ûbêt ʾābîk, "forget your own people and your father's house") — the call to transfer primary loyalty from birth-family to the new royal household — the same structure as the Davidic covenant's call to leave the old identity.
11: וְיִתְאָו הַמֶּלֶךְ יָפְיֵךְ (wĕyitʾāw hammelek yāpyêk, "Then the king will desire your beauty") — ʾāwāh (hithpael: to long for, to desire): the king's desire for the bride who has transferred her loyalty. כִּי-הוּא אֲדֹנַיִךְ וְהִשְׁתַּחֲוִי-לוֹ (kî-hûʾ ʾădōnayik wĕhishtaḥăwî-lô, "because he is your lord, bow down to him") — the structural hierarchy: the bride's lord (ʾādôn) whom she acknowledges through the act of prostration.
12: וּבַת-צֹר בְּמִנְחָה פָנַיִךְ יְחַלּוּ (ûbat-ṣōr bĕminḥāh pānayik yĕḥallû, "The daughter of Tyre comes bearing a gift") — bat-ṣōr: "daughter of Tyre" — a representative of the wealthy trading nations coming as a suppliant. Pānayik yĕḥallû: "they will seek your favor" (literally "make your face gracious") — the international acknowledgment of the queen's standing. The nations bringing tribute to the royal court, as in Psalms 72:10-11.
13: כָּל-כְּבוּדָּה בַת-מֶלֶךְ פְּנִימָה (kol-kĕbûddāh bat-melek pĕnîmāh, "The king's daughter is altogether beautiful within") — kābôd: weight, honor, beauty (the divine glory-word applied to the inner beauty of the royal bride). Pĕnîmāh: inwardly, within — the beauty is interior, the kābôd is found in the inner person. מִמִּשְׁבְּצוֹת זָהָב לְבוּשָׁהּ (mimmishbĕṣôt zāhāb lĕbûshāh, "her garment is interwoven with gold") — mishbĕṣôt: settings, inlaid work (from shābaṣ: to interweave, to set in gold). The outer garment as the visible expression of the inner beauty.
14: לִרְקָמוֹת תּוּבַל לַמֶּלֶךְ (lirqāmôt tûbal lammelek, "In embroidered garments she will be led to the king") — riqmāh: embroidery, variegated work (the multi-colored woven garment as a sign of status — Joseph's coat, Genesis 37:3, uses the same root). בְּתוּלוֹת אַחֲרֶיהָ רֵעוֹתֶיהָ (bĕtûlôt ʾaḥărêhā rêʿôtêhā, "the maidens, her companions, will follow her") — bĕtûlôt: virgins, unmarried women. The bridal procession.
15: תּוּבַלְנָה בִּשְׂמָחֹת וָגִיל (tûbalĕnāh biśmāḥōt wāgîl, "With joy and celebration they will be led in") — śimḥāh and gîl: the twin joy-words (as in Psalms 21:1; Psalms 31:7). The bridal procession characterized by the full vocabulary of rejoicing. יְבֹאֵינָה בְּהֵיכַל מֶלֶךְ (yĕbōʾênāh bĕhêkal melek, "they will enter the king's palace") — hêkāl: palace or temple (the same word for both — the ambiguity is deliberate in the royal psalm where the king's palace and the divine dwelling are conceptually linked).
16: תַּחַת אֲבֹתֶיךָ יִהְיוּ בָנֶיךָ (taḥat ʾăbōtêkā yihyû bānêkā, "In place of your ancestors will be your sons") — the dynastic continuity: the sons who will replace the fathers in the royal line. תְּשִׁיתֵמוֹ לְשָׂרִים בְּכָל-הָאָרֶץ (tĕshîtêmô lĕśārîm bĕkol-hāʾāreṣ, "you will appoint them as princes throughout all the earth") — śār: prince, official, ruler (from the same root as śārāh: to have power). The Davidic dynasty projected onto the whole earth — a universal Messianic horizon.
17: אַזְכִּירָה שִׁמְךָ בְּכָל-דֹּר וָדֹר (ʾazkîrāh shimkā bĕkol-dōr wādōr, "I will cause your name to be remembered in all generations") — the Psalmist-poet's vow: to perpetuate the king's shêm (name/reputation) through the generations by means of this very poem. עַל-כֵּן עַמִּים יְהוֹדוּךָ לְעֹלָם וָעֶד (ʿal-kên ʿammîm yĕhôdûkā lĕʿōlām wāʿed, "therefore the peoples will praise you forever and ever") — the universal praise (ʿammîm: peoples, nations) as the eschatological horizon: the king whose name is perpetuated in every generation will be praised by all nations forever. The poem itself is the vehicle of eternal remembrance — and the NT's Messianic reading of Ps 45 is exactly this perpetuation.
